text: The boiling point of alcohols is much higher than alkanes of similar molecular weight because:
options: Covalent bonding is stronger in alcohols
explanation: Alcohols form intermolecular H-bonds (O–H···O) → extra energy needed to separate molecules → much higher bp than alkanes or ethers.
name: High BP of Alcohols

text: The acidity order of alcohols and phenol is:
explanation: Acidity: PhOH(pKa ~10) > 1°ROH(~16) > 2°ROH(~17) > 3°ROH(~18). Phenoxide ion stabilised by resonance with aromatic ring.
name: Acidity Order

text: Lucas test distinguishes between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols using:
explanation: Lucas test: 3° alcohol → immediate turbidity (SN1, stable 3° carbocation). 2° → turbidity in 5 min. 1° → no turbidity at room temperature.
name: Lucas Test

text: Victor Meyer's test is used to distinguish between:
explanation: Victor Meyer's test: 1° → red, 2° → blue, 3° → colourless. Based on reaction with HNO₂ then FeCl₃ (different nitrous acid products).
name: Victor Meyer's Test

text: Dehydration of alcohols to alkenes requires:
options: Conc. H₂SO₄ at ~170°C (excess acid, high temperature)
explanation: Dehydration (elimination): alcohol + conc. H₂SO₄ at 170°C → alkene (excess H₂SO₄). At 140°C with 2 mol alcohol → ether (Williamson synthesis).
name: Dehydration of Alcohols

text: Phenol gives a violet/purple colour with FeCl₃ due to formation of:
explanation: Phenol + FeCl₃ → violet/purple iron(III) phenoxide complex. Used as a test for phenols. Enols also give positive test.
name: FeCl₃ Test for Phenol

text: The Williamson synthesis of ethers involves:
options: Reaction of sodium alkoxide with alkyl halide (SN2)
explanation: Williamson synthesis: R–O⁻Na⁺ + R'–X → R–O–R' + NaX. Best route for unsymmetrical ethers. SN2 mechanism.
name: Williamson Synthesis

text: The reaction of phenol with Br₂ water (without catalyst) gives:
explanation: Phenol is highly activated toward EAS. Br₂/water (no catalyst needed): gives 2,4,6-tribromophenol (white ppt). Used as test for phenol.
name: Bromination of Phenol

text: Which reagent is used to oxidise primary alcohol to aldehyde without over-oxidising to carboxylic acid?
explanation: PCC (CH₂Cl₂, mild): oxidises 1° alcohol → aldehyde (stops there). Strong oxidants (KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇) → carboxylic acid.
name: Selective Oxidation to Aldehyde

text: Ethers are cleaved by concentrated HI or HBr (not HCl) because:
options: I⁻ and Br⁻ are better nucleophiles than Cl⁻
Ethers are stable to HCl only
explanation: Ether cleavage: R–O–R + HI → R–OH + R–I. I⁻ is a better nucleophile than Cl⁻. HI > HBr > HCl in ether cleavage.
name: Ether Cleavage

text: The oxidation product of secondary alcohol is:
explanation: 1° alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid. 2° alcohol → ketone (cannot be further oxidised easily). 3° alcohol → resistant to oxidation.
name: Oxidation of Secondary Alcohol

text: Kolbe's reaction (Kolbe-Schmitt) converts sodium phenoxide to:
explanation: Kolbe-Schmitt: sodium phenoxide + CO₂ (high pressure, 125°C) → sodium salicylate → salicylic acid (aspirin precursor).
name: Kolbe's Reaction

text: Reimer-Tiemann reaction introduces a _____ group into phenol:
explanation: Reimer-Tiemann reaction: phenol + CHCl₃ + KOH → salicylaldehyde (2-hydroxybenzaldehyde). CHO group introduced at ortho position.
name: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction

text: The iodoform test (CHI₃, yellow precipitate) is positive for:
explanation: Positive iodoform test: CH₃COR (methyl ketones), CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol), CH₃CHOH–R (secondary alcohols with CH₃ adjacent). Gives CHI₃ (yellow).
name: Iodoform Test

text: The conversion of phenol to cyclohexanol requires:
explanation: Phenol → cyclohexanol by catalytic hydrogenation: PhOH + 3H₂ (Pt/Pd, high pressure) → cyclohexanol. The aromatic ring is reduced.
name: Phenol to Cyclohexanol
